data.title: Global Ship Lease: Embedded Upside From Charter Repricing And Trade Disruption
data.description:
After a year of our coverage initiation (Combining Value and Growth) and a total return above 100%, we are back to comment on Global Ship Lease (GSL). Since our last update (Q3 analysis), the company reported impressive Q4 results in early March, but we expect GSL to benefit over the longer term from ongoing disruptions in global trade flows.

As a reminder, despite an impressive share price performance, we have maintained a high-conviction positive supported by 1) strong operational delivery, 2) rapid deleveraging, and 3) a clearly shareholder-focused capital return policy. Yet, the stock continues to trade at an undemanding valuation. In our last analysis, we also identified incremental upside from regulatory changes in the U.S. that limit Chinese leasing competition, as well as higher secondhand vessel prices, both of which enhance the underlying asset base.

Fig 1

Why are we still positive?

Firstly, Global Ship Lease released its Q4 and FY 2025 results during the Middle East crisis (Fig. 2). Here at the Lab, we believe GSL will be a clear beneficiary of this escalation. The disruption in the Middle East has triggered a sharp increase in Time Charter Equivalent (TCE) rates across shipping segments. We are talking about oil and gas carriers, but even container charter rates are firming due to vessel shortages and higher tonne-mile demand. According to a Freightos update, there is a 5-10% price hike in TCE. Additionally, another impact is the vessel shortage. This is due to longer rerouting, port delays, and risk constraints. This has reduced available fleet capacity, tightening the charter market (and again) will lead to higher TCE rates. GSL’s earnings are highly sensitive to charter rate increases; however, the company has 99% contracted vessels in 2026 and 81% in 2027. Therefore, ~19% of the fleet still needs to be repriced for next year. This means that GSL has no upside on EBITDA uplift for this year. However, the company trades at a depressed valuation, with an EV/EBITDA of 4x or less and an asset value of its vessels in operation ($1.9 billion) below its current market cap ($1.4 billion).

Fig 2

Secondly, and still related to our evidence, last year we reported how “Liberation Day” tariff announcements would create volatility in trade. In a challenging time, we believe top-tier container liner companies will secure GSL service. This is due to a longer wait for newbuild deliveries and the flexibility to avoid committing investment CAPEX to purchasing a ship. The asset-light approach among GSL’s customers is set to persist, and these recurring distribution events create a virtuous cycle for the company.

In GSL history, the company's leverage has always been a headwind. This was due not only to GSL's valuation but also to a lower per-day break-even rate. The TCE per day price is a ratio that calculates daily revenue performance by "subtracting voyage expenses from voyage revenue and dividing by the total voyage duration." In the expenses, interests are included. The company's net debt reached $695 million (Fig. 3), with EBITDA of $521.4 million. This is very supportive and will be a clear benefit in the future.

Fig 3

Earnings Changes And Valuation

Last time, we forecasted an average 2026 rate of $30k per TCE per day. This resulted in a turnover of $730 million with an EBITDA of $535 million. Considering the latest vessels in operation, the company has already reported higher sales in 2025, with $766.5 million in results. Considering no additional investments, and 99% of sales covered, GSL's top-line sales might reach $820 million. We should report that the company has limited OPEX and minimal SG&A. Applying an EBITDA margin (aligned with 2025 results) of 68%, we reached a margin of $557.6 million. Including interest payment ($20 million), and D&A of $120 million (there is limited CAPEX guidance aligned with historical average and adjusted for annualized inflation - Fig 4), our net income reached $417.6 million. As a reminder, GSL is a Bermuda-resident company and does not pay corporate tax. Assuming no buyback, GSL's current shares outstanding are 35.92 million, so we derive an EPS of $11.6. Continuing to apply a 5-year P/E average of 3.6x, our new target price should be $41.8, representing a 9% capital appreciation opportunity. This is a company that has purchased $150 million in new vessels per year (2/3 units) and has demonstrated strong execution and a deleverage story. Last time, we included a statement that we continue to see fair, and we reiterate today, "We don't want to play with a higher target P/E, but we believe it is well justified, so we leave it to our readers to decide. We still maintain our long position."

Fig 4

Risks And Threats

GSL's downside risks include trade disruptions that could trigger force majeure among its clients. The company might be subject to a slowdown in the container shipping industry's growth. Regarding the company, we see risks from an aging fleet and higher maintenance costs due to CAPEX constraints. Additionally, we see risks in client concentration (Technomar) and in higher regulatory requirements. The company is subject to negative global events. The Strait of Hormuz has a limited impact on GSL; however, potential disruptions to shipping routes, such as the Suez Canal, could affect the company's P&L.

Conclusion

All in all, we believe the company will continue to deliver and, sooner rather than later, be recognized by Wall Street. Even if GSL has limited earnings upside, the company has secured contracts for the next few years, and this should be reflected in the valuation. Therefore, we remain buyers.

data.title: Marvell: Rating Upgrade On Data Center Boom
data.description:
By Anthony Goh, Senior Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

In our previous analysis, we examined Marvell’s (MRVL) Data Center segment and believed its growth would be driven primarily by custom AI ASICs as hyperscalers increasingly develop their own accelerators. We highlighted Marvell’s partnerships with Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT) and expected the ramp of next-generation chips such as AWS Trainium and Microsoft Maia to support growth. In addition, we noted its broad interconnect portfolio and Ethernet switching products such as Teralynx, which could benefit from rising AI infrastructure demand and hyperscaler efforts to diversify suppliers.

Since then, Marvell’s stock has risen 100% and has surpassed our previous price target. Its FY2026 revenue and margins also exceeded our expectations, with revenue growth of 42.09% and a net margin of 32.58%. Thus, we revisit the company to determine whether its stronger outlook supports a higher valuation. We first focus on its growth by analyzing the Data Center segment, which accounts for 74% of its revenue and grew by 46% YoY, with guidance for FY2027 at 40%.

We analyze its revenue segments within the Data Center and their growth, particularly custom AI accelerators, optical interconnects, and switching solutions. In addition, we also investigate whether the company’s product developments and acquisitions further support its Data Center growth outlook. In terms of profitability, we examine its margins and trends, especially FY2026, to analyze the factors for the large change in margin and update our projections.

Stronger Data Center Segment Growth

In the first section, we examine Marvell's revenue growth, focusing on the Data Center segment, to analyze the drivers by product segments behind its strong growth. We compile the breakdown based on disclosures from its past earnings transcripts and estimates where figures were not clearly disclosed. This is different compared to our previous analysis, where we derived an estimate of its product segment breakdown based on the weighted breakdown of its investor presentation TAM. The company has also reorganized its non-Data Center segment into Communications and others.

Marvell's total Data Center segment grew by 46.5% in FY2026, which is higher than our previous forecast of 32.3%. Based on the table, the Data Center segment is split into 4 product categories, which are custom AI accelerators, optical interconnects, switching solutions, and Storage & others. The largest product segment is Interconnects, where Marvell is strong with products like PAM4 and optical DSPs that are needed to connect server racks within data centers. Based on the Q4 FY2025 transcript, management indicated that electro-optics is about half of Data Center revenue. Applying this ratio implies around $3 billion in revenue, which is higher than our previous analysis ($1.5 billion) based on the prorating of its TAM. This implies strong growth of around 47% for Interconnects. Management's guidance of 50% growth for FY2027 also points to continued strong growth, which is higher than the 35% CAGR from its Interconnect TAM presentation.

The second largest product category is custom AI accelerators, which involves designing custom ASIC chips for data center customers to have their own in-house chips, particularly Amazon and Microsoft. Management stated it doubled in FY2026 to $1.5 billion, which is more than our previous estimate of $1.1 billion. The FY2027 growth guidance is only 20% for the segment, but management also guided FY2028 to double. One factor could be due to the “lumpiness” we highlighted in our previous analysis, as revenues depend on the timing of customer purchases and may not be consistent. This is also still a relatively new emerging segment for Marvell.

Another emerging segment is Switching, which relates to Ethernet switch chips, where Broadcom currently dominates. Marvell's revenue disclosed by management is only a mere $300 million here in FY2026, but its strong guidance to double in FY2027 indicates its expansion into the switching market is gaining momentum. The last segment within Data Center is Storage & Other, which has a better implied growth rate of 25% in FY2027 based on its Data Center guidance, but this mainly represents a recovery compared to the previous year. Management’s TAM CAGR for the Storage market is only 9%, making it the lowest growth segment among the others. Besides the Data Center, Marvell has reorganized its Communications & Other segment by combining the previous Enterprise Networking, Carrier Infrastructure, Consumer, and Automotive/Industrial segments. This segment showed strong growth in FY2026 due to end-market recovery, as we previously covered, and is guided to grow 10% in FY2027, which is also in line with our previous projections.

We overall update our revenue projection for Marvell's Data Center segment in the table above based on our compiled figures, which are more representative and reflective of its actual Data Center breakdown instead of a simple TAM prorating method from our previous analysis.

For the Custom AI Accelerators, we assume management’s growth guidance of 20% in CY2026 (FY2027), which represents a slowdown compared to 2025 and could be due to the timing of custom ASIC orders. However, we also forecast Marvell to grow strongly from 2027 onward, outpacing our ASIC market growth CAGR estimate of 52.9%, as we continue to see its market share rising while its major customers, Amazon and Microsoft, the two largest cloud companies, scale their custom chips with Marvell.

Our 2028 Custom revenue of $7.9 billion is also more conservative than management's target of 20% market share on a $55.4 billion Custom TAM, which we assume, given increasing competitive dynamics, which we explain in the next section. For Switching, we apply management's guidance of 100% in 2026 and base 2027 onwards on our data center market projections as a conservative estimate, as Marvell is still very new in the segment and competes against a strong, dominant Broadcom (AVGO) that has a switch chip breadth advantage, as covered in our previous analysis.

For Interconnects, we apply the forecast CAGR of 35% based on its market TAM presentation as a conservative assumption, whereas for Storage, we calculate 2026 based on the implied growth from the remainder Data Center segment guidance and the Storage market forecast CAGR of 9.1% through 2029. For the non-Data Center segment, which is Communications and others and has been combined, we continue to base it on our updated previous forecasts, with 2026 in line with management's guidance of 10%. All in all, we see the company growing 32.5% this year, slower than the previous year due to moderation in Custom revenue, but see an acceleration through 2028, supported by the Interconnect segment, which is also high growth.

Data Center-Related Product Developments and Acquisitions

Next, we examine Marvell's stated product developments and acquisitions in the recent quarter, focusing on the company’s two main high-growth Data Center product categories, which are interconnects and custom AI accelerators. For Ethernet switching, we highlighted in our previous analysis that Broadcom remains the dominant player due to its much larger product portfolio, but Marvell could benefit as customers seek to diversify and reduce reliance on Broadcom, although Broadcom is expected to remain the main player.

Based on the comparison table above of the top players in networking semiconductors, Marvell has a broad interconnect portfolio, with a presence across all key categories, including PAM4 optical DSPs, AEC/copper DSPs, coherent DSPs, silicon photonics/PICs, co-packaged optics (CPO), and PCIe/CXL retimers. Since our previous analysis, Marvell has also introduced several new product developments. This includes Marvell’s 1.6T AEC DSPs, which management said have secured design wins from cloud hyperscalers. Its PCIe Gen6 retimers (Alaska P) are also gaining traction with multiple design-ins and are expected to enter production soon, according to management. In CPO, it is integrating silicon photonics into AI accelerators supported by the Celestial AI acquisition. In terms of its roadmap, Marvell emphasizes the development of 400G-per-lane PAM, supporting 3.2T optics compared to the current 1.6T generation, as well as continued development in coherent and silicon photonics. This shows its commitment to maintaining competitiveness in data center interconnects.

For Custom AI Accelerators, which we focused on in our previous analysis, we continue to expect Marvell's position here to remain supported by its custom ASIC development partnerships with Amazon and Microsoft. In the previous earnings transcripts, Marvell indicated that its “Lead XPU program” is “doing well” and that the company has “secured 3-nanometer wafer and advanced packaging capacity and expects to start production in calendar 2026.”

While reports indicate that ALCHIP (ALCPF) is also reportedly chosen by Amazon to diversify and reduce reliance on Marvell for Trainium 3 and 4, Marvell has a 5-year agreement with Amazon covering custom AI chips as well as interconnects. Given Amazon being the largest cloud provider and having strong demand for its chips by Anthropic, which had committed to training new models using its chips and secured 500,000 Trainium2 chips through Project Rainier, we believe Marvell is still well-positioned to benefit from Amazon.

Marvell also referred in its calls to its second customer, which could be Microsoft, where management mentioned that “joint development on this program continues to progress well,” and this ties in with Microsoft having released the 3nm Maia 200 AI accelerator earlier this year. Management also highlighted it is “already engaged” on the architecture for the follow-on generation, which could hint at the next-generation Maia 300 and tie into the expected ramp in FY2028.

In terms of acquisitions, the company has completed 2 deals. The first of which is Celestial AI, an interconnect company specializing in scale-up optical interconnects and photonic fabric chiplets (PF chiplets). According to Marvell, its products are used by cloud hyperscalers, thus overlapping with its interconnects portfolio. Management expects its revenue to reach “a $500 million annualized run rate in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2028, doubling to a $1 billion run rate by the fourth quarter of fiscal 2029.” The other acquisition is XConn, which complements its data center Switching business with PCIe Gen6 and CXL 3.1 switches and had more than 20 customers prior to the acquisition. In total, these deals are expected to provide a $250 million revenue contribution in FY2028, which is still very minor at only 1% of our total Marvell revenue forecast, thus indicating these expansions are still in very early stages. Management did mention they expect Celestial AI’s revenue run rate to double to $1 billion by Q4 FY2029, which shows a strong ramp-up expected but depends on execution. The silicon photonics and hybrid switch markets have forecast CAGRs of 25.3% and 12.3%, respectively.

Looking ahead, we believe in the main segments within Data Center, which are Interconnects and Custom AI accelerators. Marvell is positioned well and is important, as both of these account for 75% of total Data Center segment revenue, and our forecasts show it rising to 87.7% by 2029. The strongest we see is in interconnects, which is the largest contributor to its data center segment revenue and is the second fastest growing based on its investor presentation TAM growth and our forecasts. This is as we see its broad portfolio as a key strength to capitalize on the growth of data center infrastructure requiring greater connectivity, supported by the company’s continued developments and roadmap, such as 3.2T optical DSPs for rack-to-rack communications. In custom AI accelerators, while there is reported competition from players like ALCHIP, especially with Amazon, we expect Marvell’s growth to remain positive even if it does not secure all of the share of orders, as Amazon and Microsoft combined represent 49% of cloud market share, while our forecast for Marvell only assumes it reaches 10% market share of the custom ASIC market in 2028, which we forecast at $78.7 billion. The acquisitions of Celestial AI and XConn seem interesting but are still clearly in early stages and unlikely to boost revenues much in the near term, as the guidance of $250 million only represents 1% of revenue.

Reason for Improved Margins and Profitability Outlook

Moving on to the profitability of the company, its FY2026 net margin came in stronger than we anticipated at 32.58%, much higher compared to our forecast of -2.96% in our previous analysis. Thus, we analyze its earnings and margins chart below to observe its trends.

Marvell’s margins have generally been negative but show a volatile trend. Its net margins have been negative since 2020 and had an outlier surge in CY2025. The reason for this surge in net margin is due to the inclusion of a one-off gain from “the sale of its automotive ethernet business to Infineon Technologies AG (IFNNY) for $2.5 billion in cash”, which boosted its net margin by 22.3%. A similar reason is also the case for the 2019 bump, as it recorded a gain on sale of divestiture of $1.12 billion, which relates to the sale of its Wi-Fi connectivity business. This is the main reason for the large difference in the margin, which also boosted its FCF margin based on total investing cash flow with the $2.5 billion in proceeds. That said, excluding the divestiture effect, we calculate it would have a net margin of 10.25%, still better than our previous forecast, and more importantly, its core business has indeed turned profitable on a net margin basis.

A major reason for the improvement in its net margin is mainly due to its strong revenue growth in CY2025. As revenues grew strongly by 42%, its largest operating expenses, such as R&D, SG&A, and depreciation and amortization, did not grow as much, leading to improvement as a percentage of revenue and highlighting better operating scale.

For R&D expenses, they grew by 6.4%, driven by IP development and customer design win activity, though this was significantly lower than revenue growth, indicating operating leverage. As a chipmaker, Marvell incurs R&D upfront during development, and once products are launched, revenue can scale without a proportional increase in R&D, which is reflected in its declining R&D as a percentage of revenue, falling from 35% in 2016 to 25.2% in 2025.

For depreciation and amortization, they declined as a percentage of revenue in 2025 due to strong revenue growth and have also been stabilizing in recent years following a prior increase between 2019 and 2021, driven by acquisitions such as Inphi (2021) and others, which led to higher amortization expenses.

In terms of SG&A, it declined by 3.9%, while its number of employees only grew by 6.22%, as the company previously implemented restructuring and cost-saving initiatives. In Q3 FY2025, Marvell initiated a corporate restructuring plan involving targeted workforce reductions and realignment of business units. It also reallocated resources by cutting costs in weaker segments, such as Carrier Infrastructure and Enterprise Networking, and redirecting them towards Data Center.

Even without the one-off gain, we view Marvell’s profitability improvement as a positive development, mainly driven by its strong revenue growth and improving operating scale. We expect the company’s margins to continue improving, supported by continued strong growth in its Data Center segment and further scale benefits. We model its net margin to be 13.61% in 2026 and rising to 20.75% by 2030, based on our assumptions of its key operating expenses. This includes R&D declining slightly as a percentage of revenue in line with its long-term trend, from 25.32% in 2026 to 24.17%; depreciation and amortization based on its annual report amortization schedule and historical depreciation as a percentage of fixed assets; and SG&A based on its 3-year average. More importantly, we see FCF margins remaining positive and strong, with a 5-year forward average of 25%, higher than our previous analysis of a 5-year forward average of 17.8% due to factoring in higher operating margins.

Risk: Development of Alternative Interconnects

One key risk for Marvell is the development of alternative interconnect technologies, such as LPO. Traditional pluggable optics uses high-end DSP chips to clean and retime signals over longer distances, whereas LPO removes most of the DSP layer and instead uses a simpler analog system, which may lower power consumption and reduce latency. This is attractive for short-reach links inside AI data centers. That said, LPO has limitations like shorter reach and may not be a full replacement for all optical solutions. Marvell does have exposure to LPO, but it could also pose a cannibalization risk to its main DSP products.

Valuation

We incorporate our revised revenue and margin projections, which are stronger than our previous analysis, and update our DCF valuation of Marvell. Based on a discount rate of 12.4% (company’s WACC), with beta based on the top chipmakers’ 5-year average of 1.39 and a terminal value based on the 5-year average EV/EBITDA multiple of top chipmakers of 23.56x, our model shows an upside of 60.92%.

Verdict

Overall, we expect Marvell’s growth outlook to remain strong, supported by its high exposure to Data Center demand, particularly in interconnects and custom AI accelerators, where we believe the company has key strengths driven by its broad connectivity portfolio and deep hyperscaler partnerships with customers such as Amazon and Microsoft. While custom AI silicon remains a core growth driver, we increasingly see interconnect as a major contributor given its larger role across the AI infrastructure stack and strong growth outlook. At the same time, we expect margins to continue improving as revenue scales, supported by operating leverage and declining cost intensity. Based on our DCF valuation, we rate the company as a Strong Buy, with a much higher price target of $184.17 compared to $80.24 previously, reflecting our revised 5-year forward growth of 34%, driven not only by strong growth in AI accelerators but also interconnects, alongside improving profitability as the company scales.

data.title: EMCOR Group: Solid Business That Is Fully Valued
data.description:
Executive Summary

EMCOR Group (EME) is one of the largest providers of electrical and mechanical construction services in the U.S. The company recently reported a solid 2025 4th quarter with revenues hitting $4.51 billion (beating estimates by $229.96 million) for the quarter and $16.99 billion (beating the top-end management guidance of $16.8 billion) for the full year. This was a handsome beat and a great year for EME.

Based on EME's stock price (~$787 at the time of me writing this article) and based on the company's valuation (more on this later), I am initiating my coverage with a HOLD rating. Yes, there are tailwinds, and the company is expected to have a stellar 2-3 years, but the price seems to be at fair value here.

Company Overview

As this is my first coverage of the company, I would like to spend some time reviewing the company's segments and providing some thoughts on the sectors that it serves.

The company had a U.K. arm, which it successfully sold off in Dec 2025. So, going forward, the company will operate only within the U.S. Well, some respite from tariff risks.

The company has 4 core segments: Mechanical Construction, Electrical Construction, Building Services, and Industrial Services. It is interesting to note that a surge in business within the construction segment could benefit the building services segment. The company drives a lot of organic growth in this matter. The below is a snapshot of the company's current business landscape:

The key takeaway from the above graphics is that the company's remaining performance obligations (RPOs) have gone up significantly over the past few years, and Network and Communications has been a primary driver of this growth. However, it is important that readers do not attribute all this to just data centers.

RPOs reached a massive $13.25 billion by 12/31/2025. Readers need to know that these amounts are contractual amounts, and the company doesn't report anything that is not in the contract.

A lot of analysts (including yours truly) are attributing growth in companies to data center spending. But companies like Emcor are experiencing growth in many other sectors.

The following set of graphics will bring my point home. The chart bifurcates the growth in backlogs by end market sectors.

You will notice that the fastest-growing sector is the Network & Communications (57%), which is primarily driven by Data Center buildouts. But that's not the only theme driving growth. Almost the entire end market is growing at a double-digit CAGR except transportation. (Management has attributed the decline to slower buildouts here.) This will continue to decline unless we see activity here, such as new airports, etc.

Other themes like reshoring of manufacturing (such as new semiconductor fabs and biopharma labs) are driving the growth in the High-tech Manufacturing end market (48%).

Lastly, end markets like Water & Wastewater, Hospitality & Entertainment, Healthcare are providing stable growth, and these are something that will continue to grow with GDP.

Electrical Construction

This segment caters to electrical systems installations such as lighting, power generation, fire alarms, and security systems. This is a high-margin segment and the growth is driven by many underlying sectors. Growth in data center buildouts has helped this segment.

Mechanical Construction

This is the largest segment by revenue and is quite complex. This involves building HVAC, plumbing, clean rooms, airflow management, etc. The recent efforts with reshoring in the country have been a primary driver for growth here.

Building Services

This segment caters to ongoing facility management and building operations support functions. In fact, this often acts as the organic growth driver for the company. When the company completes an electrical/mechanical project, they tend to also win the building services contract. Well, if they built it, then they can maintain it!

Industrial Services

This segment caters to the oil industry with support for plant maintenance, cleaning services, specialty welding, maintenance of heat exchangers, towers, etc. With all the news around oil, it is not surprising that this segment is also growing impressively.

Note to Readers: The company sold off its UK segment in Dec 2025 and thus is not being covered in my article now.

Well, all this sounds good. But, I think I should also talk about the overall macro-industry before I spend time on the financials.

Industry Overview (Macro-level Picture)

Total Construction Spending - Manufacturing

Since COVID, the manufacturing construction spending in the US has skyrocketed to ~220+ billion. Although we see a trend reversal, I believe the overall spending will remain at elevated levels as compared to pre-COVID levels due to reshoring of semiconductor fabs and biopharma labs.

The above chart also reinforces the company's high CAGR in RPOs, as shown in an earlier graphic above. Emcor will be a beneficiary out of this massive spending.

Total Construction Spending - Commercial

This chart reflects the spending in the commercial construction sector that includes office space, the retail sector, warehouse construction, and also data centers. The retail sector has been weak for years, and there is not much activity in office space or warehouse construction. So a majority of the growth is coming from data centers.

If you look at the above two charts closely, you will notice a divergence. In 2004, manufacturing spending (~25 billion) was lower than commercial spending (~60 billion). In 2026, manufacturing spending (~200 billion) far outpaced commercial spending (~120 billion).

This divergence indicates that growth from data centers will improve Emcor's margins, but there are other themes that will also improve margins. This is something management talks about regularly: "We don't chase fads".

This is also important for retail investors because a slowdown in data center spending will punish the entire market, and we can opportunistically pick on good companies such as Emcor during the slowdown.

Total Construction Spending - Transportation

I included the above chart to show that the growth (absolute growth percentage number) in transportation spending has remained flat (thus the lack of parabolic moves like the other two charts).

This is why transportation has been a laggard for the company. However, the bright spot is transportation-related revenue is a small part of the company's overall revenue pie.

All Employees - Specialty Trade Contractors

The above two are the final charts that I want to review. If you notice, the percentage change from last year is relatively flat. What this means is that even though the labor market for specialty trade contractors is at near all-time-highs, the labor market is constrained.

This benefits Emcor because the company has always been seen as the "employer of choice". Thus, competitors are unlikely to find and hire talent.

This is all great. So what's the catch? - Price! Emcor is priced to perfection.

Fundamentals

Income Statement Forecast

The company's revenue has grown at 16.2% CAGR for the past 3 years, and I have projected this to slow down to 6.4% for the next 5 years. This is based on management's guidance for 2026 and maintaining a similar growth rate for the next few years.

The spending is slowing down, as seen from the total manufacturing and commercial spending charts shown earlier. This will reflect in a slowdown to their explosive 16% CAGR.

Even a 5-year forecast of 6.4% CAGR in the construction industry is very impressive and showcases that Emcor is not a cyclical business and is a beneficiary of many secular trends.

Drivers

I already provided insights on growth forecasts. My model is projecting an average 6.4% growth in the next few years, which is supported by data center buildouts, building new semiconductor facilities, and growth from other stable sectors like healthcare, water treatment, and institutional (i.e., colleges and universities).

The company operates in a complex environment, especially related to construction. Margins are always tight. The company has historically ensured margin protection through executing projects exquisitely. Thus, I am projecting that margins will remain within 19.4%-19.6% for the next 5 years.

Similarly, I have maintained SG&A between 9.9% and 10%.

The below is a summary of my income statement core drivers

Capital Structure

One thing I admire about this company is their ability to maintain very low leverage. The company has no debt and has a small portion of lease liability. They have $1,112 billion in cash and about $6 million in debt-like instruments, resulting in a net debt of approximately -$1,105 million.

This is something management highlights in all their calls. Management is also not willing to issue debt to repurchase stock. Instead, the company issues dividends and is repurchasing its shares to increase shareholder returns. The company increased its dividend by 60% to 40.40 in Q1 2026.

The following is a snapshot of the company's capital allocation:

My CAPEX allocation for the next 5 years is projected to be between 0.6% to 0.7% of revenue, which is in line with management expectations as well.

And really, what we've seen is still a capital-light business for us. Our CapEx is like 0.6%, 0.7% of revenues.

DCF

Please let me know if you have any questions regarding my assumptions building this DCF model.

WACC

I caution readers when it comes to WACC. The model is highly sensitive to WACC and the perpetuity growth rates. So it becomes prudent to do a sensitivity analysis.

WACC Assumptions

I selected a target net-debt-to-total capitalization as 1% since the company historically has a negative net debt.

I selected Risk Free Rate as 4.35%, and the Market Risk Premium was selected as 4.46%.

The beta was selected as 1.11.

Valuation

With a 3% perpetuity growth rate and WACC of 9.3%, we get a fair value of $618 using the perpetuity method. Similarly, an exit EBITDA multiple of 14x and a WACC of 9.3% yield a share price of $707.

The market is rewarding Emcor with a higher multiple for its exposure to data center buildout and is typically not valuing this as a traditional construction business. Thus, it deserves a higher multiple. How much?

Let's perform a sensitivity analysis:

Sensitivity Analysis

Even taking up the perpetuity growth rate to 4% and reducing WACC to 8.8% gives us $774. And increasing the exit EBITDA multiple to 16x and 9.3% WACC gives us $783, and the stock price is currently trading at $802 at the time of writing this article.

Valuation Summary

In my opinion, the attractive share price range is between $650 and $750. As the stock is trading at $802 at the time of this writing, I am inclined to issue a HOLD rating.

Bear Case

We are noticing a slowdown in overall construction spending, as shown in my FRED charts earlier. Any acceleration will have a direct impact on the company's revenue. However, this will first reflect in a slowdown in the company's RPO.

Growth Rate Stagnation

You see, growth from high-tech manufacturing was up 48% CAGR from 2019 to 2025, primarily driven by reshoring of semiconductor fabrication facilities. This is a major theme going forward, but this will slow down in the near future. I have accounted for all this by reducing the revenue growth from 16.2% historical CAGR to about 6.7%.

Labor Market

The growth in highly skilled specialty trade contractors has stalled (although at near all-time highs). The company might find it difficult to hire labor, causing a slowdown in project progress and thereby cash collected from these projects.

GDP

Justifying a growth rate higher than GDP for a construction business is difficult, although I have stated my opinion on why the company deserves higher growth rates. However, at the end of the day, will the market continue to accept a higher growth rate for EME? Only time will tell.

Conclusion

This is a solid company with a very capable management team. The company is exposed to some of the fastest-growing sectors and is a prime beneficiary of AI data center buildouts. However, as a fundamental analyst, I must accept the fact that the train has left the station, and I should wait for better entry points. Thus, I initiate my coverage with a HOLD rating.

data.title: High-Yield REITs I Would Trust For Retirement Income
data.description:
REIT ETFs do not offer particularly high dividend yields.

The iShares US Real Estate ETF (IYR) only pays a 2.3% dividend yield.

The State Street Real Estate ETF (XLRE) pays a 3.4% dividend yield.

And the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) pays a 3.8% dividend yield.

These relatively low yields lead many retirees to conclude that REITs aren't lucrative enough for them and will instead focus more on BDCs (BIZD) and MLPs (AMLP).

But in reality, the yields of these REIT ETFs are only so low because they are dominated by mega-cap growth REITs with low leverage, focusing on low-yielding sectors like data centers, and retaining a big chunk of their cash flow for growth.

Remember that REITs must pay out 90% of their "taxable income" in the form of dividends, but that is far lower than the actual cash flows of REITs due to non-cash depreciation. For this reason, some REITs like SBA Communications (SBAC), pay out as little as 40% of their cash flows.

What this means is that if you want to earn a higher yield from REITs, it is certainly possible, but it requires an active strategy.

You would need to focus on those REITs that own higher-yielding properties and distribute a greater share of their cash flows to their shareholders.

At the same time, you, of course, would not want to buy just any high-yielding REIT, especially in retirement, as many REITs are overleveraged, poorly managed, and may not sustain their dividends.

In what follows, I am going to highlight some high-yielding REITs, ranked from the lowest to the highest yielder, that I would trust for my retirement income.

Their average dividend yield is about 6%, and their dividends are well-covered with a low 70-80% payout ratio and backed by strong balance sheets with low leverage.

NNN REIT (NNN): 5.7% Dividend Yield

NNN is a no-brainer for this list. The REIT has the second-longest dividend growth track record of the entire REIT sector at 36 years, resisting the dot-com crash, the Great Financial Crisis, the pandemic, and the surge in interest rates of recent years:

Today, the REIT is better positioned than ever to keep this going, owning the biggest and best diversified portfolio in its history, having a strong BBB+ rated balance sheet with exceptionally long debt maturities at 12 years, and retaining 30% of its cash flow to reinvest in growth - a historically low payout ratio.

The REIT may not enjoy explosive growth, but its service-oriented net lease properties generate highly consistent and predictable cash flow from 10+-year-long leases with steady annual rent escalations, which, coupled with its strong balance sheet and new acquisitions, should allow it to keep growing at 3-5% per year going forward.

Its valuation is also historically low, trading at just 12x FFO, offering a margin of safety and future upside potential if and when interest rates return to lower levels.

CubeSmart (CUBE): 5.8% Dividend Yield

CUBE is one of the leading self-storage REITs, and it has historically typically traded at closer to a 4% dividend yield because this is a sector that generally trades at high valuations due to its strong growth potential and high returns.

Yet, today you can buy it at a near 6% dividend yield because its valuation has dropped to a near-decade low level due to temporary oversupply:

The pandemic led to some bumpiness, with demand first spiking, attracting a lot of developers who started building all over the place. But the world then normalized, and demand cooled down right as these new properties started hitting the market.

This has put pressure on occupancy rates and rent growth in recent years, depressing the market sentiment of self-storage REITs.

But the good news is that this is not the end of the world for a REIT like CUBE, and besides, we are now seeing light at the end of the tunnel.

CUBE has a strong BBB balance sheet and some of the best assets in its peer group.

As a result, it has managed to maintain its cash flow and keep growing its dividend throughout this self-storage bear market.

And growth is expected to accelerate already in the near term as the supply now gets absorbed, and the AI-driven labor market disruption could increase mobility, resulting in more demand for self-storage.

The big private equity players like Brookfield (BAM) and Blackstone (BX) know this, which is why they have been going after self-storage REITs lately.

Trading at a historically low valuation, right as fundamentals are about to improve, it would not surprise me if CUBE also became a buyout target. But even if it doesn't, it could enjoy significant upside potential over the coming years as its valuation recovers closer to where it used to be.

Sila Realty Trust (SILA): 6.7% Dividend Yield

Now, we move to a near 7% dividend yield, and with that, naturally, comes higher risk. SILA is a smaller REIT that came public just recently.

But I think that its dividend is quite a bit safer than what the market gives it credit for.

The REIT specializes in healthcare properties such as medical office buildings, inpatient rehabilitation centers, and surgical facilities, all leased on a triple net basis with 10+-year-long leases and steady annual rent escalations.

Importantly, its tenants are highly profitable, enjoying a near 6x rent coverage ratio, resulting in a significant margin of safety.

The REIT also has low leverage with a 3.9x debt-to-EBITDA. This is one of the lowest of any healthcare or net lease REIT. For comparison, Realty Income (O) has a 5.4x debt-to-EBITDA.

Finally, the REIT retains about 30% of its cash flow to keep reinvesting in growth, which, coupled with its near 2% annual rent escalations, should result in 3-5% annual growth over time and allow the REIT to keep hiking its dividend.

The combination of a low payout ratio, low leverage, and steady cash flow and growth is exceptional for a REIT offering such a high yield, making it a great pick for more conservative income-seeking investors.

Investor Takeaway

There are, of course, even higher-yielding REITs that could fit a retirement portfolio. Some REITs offer as much as 10% dividend yields that we believe to be sustainable and even growing in select cases.

The point of this article was to show you that the REIT market can offer steadily growing 6%+ yielding opportunities, and the REIT ETFs are not reflective of the average REIT as they are dominated by low-yielding growth REITs.

At High Yield Landlord, we look for those REITs that offer sustainable and growing 6% yields and then invest in them as part of our active portfolio that seeks to beat the market.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rubrik's Growth Engines Are Working, But 18% Dilution Risk Weighs On The Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Rubrik Inc. (RBRK) is down 35% YTD, mainly due to the overall software industry’s sell-off and not because of a slowdown in the company’s performance.

Many investors and analysts are focusing on the company’s Q4’26 revenue and ARR growth, as well as the impressive FCF increase. In my view, FCF does not reflect RBRK’s full financial performance, as it hides a major risk factor that investors need to take into account: stock-based consumption (SBC).

Additionally, I’m trying to analyze the company’s current momentum, given that the core business - cloud subscription revenue from data reliance and backup solutions -seems mature, and the promising growth catalysts of identity security and AI agent security remain at an early stage.

The Strong ARR Revenue Growth Doesn’t Translate to Healthy FCF Given the SBC Expenses

First, let me explain why I focus on ARR!

RBRK traditionally offers data recovery and backup solutions through its Rubic Security Cloud (RSC) platform. Two years ago the company expanded into identity security solutions able to protect the most prominent ecosystem, such as Microsoft Active Directory, Microsoft Entra ID, and Okta. Recently, it developed the Rubrik Agent Cloud, which monitors and controls AI agents within an enterprise.

RBRK is a software-as-a-service (SaaS) company that generates revenue under a subscription model. This means that revenue may be affected by timing effects; therefore, revenue volatility is more likely to appear over time. For this reason, annual recurring revenue (ARR) - which represents the total annualized value of the company’s active subscriptions and reflects the practicable revenue that RBRK expects to generate over the next 12-months – is more suitable performance metric than revenue growth.

The company reported impressive ARR YoY growth of 34% in Q4’26, reaching almost $1.5B. Notably, the ARR YoY growth for 2025 was 39%, and the company anticipates ARR of about $1.83B in FY27, implying 22% YoY growth compared to FY26. The point is that ARR growth seems to slow down over the years and supports the CFO’s statement: “We're at the tail end of our cloud transition with cloud ARR now representing 88% of subscription ARR as of Q4.” The CFO pointed out that cloud ARR is affected by RBRK still supporting its legacy data security solutions on premises. But the big picture is that the company acknowledges that cloud data recovery and backup is a mature business line.

A key component for this growth is the fact that RBRK expanded rapidly among customers with ARR over $100K, with a total of 2,805 customers in financial year 2026, compared to 2,246 in FY25, representing almost 25% YoY growth. I find this growth particularly interesting, as large customers create cross-selling opportunities, as we’ll examine later.

The key point from the above is that RBRK still holds strong momentum, given the strong ARR, despite a slowdown. ARR is driven mainly by large customers, creating cross-selling opportunities. In terms of profitability, the company remains unprofitable, despite the impressive gross margin of 82% and the even more massive ARR contribution margin of 12% in FY26—up from just 2% in the previous year.

The main reason the company remains unprofitable is that it spends 58% of its revenue on marketing expenses in order to attract new customers. In total, operating expenses accounted for 106% of revenue in FY26, driving a net loss of about $349M.

However, I do not see the high marketing expenses as a risk, given that the company is fast-growing. In contrast, SBC is a major concern, in my view. SBC reached $329M in FY26, notably lower than about $914M in FY25. But let’s do the math:

  • Operating cash flow (OCF) was about $283M in FY26
  • CapEx was about $45M in FY26
  • Therefore, FCF was 238M in FY26
  • However, from the above OCF, $329M came from the SBC line, which means SBC > OCF
  • As a result, SBC led to strong FCF growth

In my opinion, the problem arises from the fact that SBC needs to be paid off at some point. Specifically, RBRK’s shares were about 196.5M in FY26, and the company anticipates 232M shares for FY27, which means a dilution of 18% in order for RBRK to pay its SBC liabilities. To remind you, SBC means that personnel, instead of salary, receive company stock.

You have probably already understood my view. SBC is an accounting line that increases FCF and does not reflect the company’s ability to convert revenue into cash. Therefore, the FCF margin of 18% does not prove an efficient and profitable business model, and I suggest investors closely monitor the FCF sources of Rubrik.

Growth Engines: Data resilience is A Relative Mature Business Line, Identity Security is Promising and AI Agent Security is at a Very Early Stage

Data recovery and backup are important after cyberattacks occur, and Rubrik’s solutions mainly don’t prevent cyberattacks but offer cyber resilience with immutable data. The company seems to be the leader in this market, based on the quadrant below. Notably, RBRK doesn’t directly compete with well-known companies such as CrowdStrike (CRWD) or Palo Alto (PANW); instead, it enhances these cybersecurity platforms as the last line of defense.

As already mentioned, based on the company’s view, the cloud data recovery and backup business has limited room for expansion. However, net new subscription ARR reached $115M in Q4’26, resulting in Rubrik Security Cloud (RSC) reaching $1.29B of cloud ARR, which increased 48% YoY, still reflecting a strong business line.

Also, as mentioned, large customers are driving ARR growth. Specifically, the company stated:

Third, our subscription NRR remained strong, once again above 120%. These large customers now represent 87% of our subscription ARR, an increase from 84% a year ago. Furthermore, we added a record 32 customers with subscription ARR of $1 million or more, driving over 50% growth in our over $1 million subscription base.

In my view, large customers create cross-selling opportunities. As evidence, the Net Retention Rate (NRR) surpasses 120%, meaning that RBRK gained 20% more revenue from its existing customers compared to the previous year.

A key business line that creates RBRK’s cross-selling opportunities is identity security solutions. Although management has not disclosed any specific financial information about how revenue or operating income the identity business line contributes, the fact that Rubrik more than doubled its clients in this business in just one quarter reflects strong momentum. Management described it as follows:

In Q3, we had reported that we had crossed 400 customers. Now we have 900 customers. And it is the fastest-growing product in the history of our company. But Identity we built 2 years ago… In fact, over 50% of Rubrik M365 bookings this quarter were attached to our identity solutions.

Moreover, half of its clients who bought RSC for Microsoft 365 also bought Identity Security. In my view, the identity security line is a more than promising growth engine. I found it interesting that RBRK’s Identity reduces Recovery Time Objective (RTO) by 86%, saving even $3M in costs for enterprises.

That said, we come to the company’s third growth catalyst, AI Agent Security, via Rubrik Agent Cloud (RAC). The company summarized RAC as follows:

Rubrik Agent Cloud is this comprehensive AI operations platform. This is the intelligent autonomous driving system that we provide, so our customers can safely drive fast with AI agents…By leveraging AI to control agentic work, agents can't leak sensitive data, can't say wrong things and can't take wrong actions. AI controlling AI agents is key…Last quarter, we shared that Rubrik Agent Cloud was in beta. Just a few weeks ago, we made Rubrik Agent Cloud generally available. And we have a number of POCs ongoing across early AI adopters as well as Fortune 500 companies.

RAC is still at a very early stage, specifically at the proof-of-concept (POC) stage; therefore, I can’t see this catalyst contributing significant revenue before FY27. As a long-term investor, I can wait to see revenue from this business line, but the crucial point is how profitable it will be and what level of demand it will experience.

In general, analysts expect AI to strongly boost the overall cybersecurity industry. In detail, Wedbush noted:

We note that today we estimate cyber security represents roughly 5% of IT budgets and will likely be doubling over the next few years to 10% as protecting the tidal wave of agents, AI use cases, and broader technology push poses massive risks to many organizations we spoke with on the floor.

Furthermore, based on RBRK, AI agents have created a massive amount of non-human identities (NHIs), at a ratio of 82-1 compared to human users, opening a new massive AI-agent identity security market. Investors need to answer the question of whether RAC can attract new customers or create further cross-selling opportunities.

Based on the current data, the answer is we don’t know!

Given that RAC was recently released, we don’t have any comparison or analysis with similar competitive products; therefore, we cannot assess RAC’s efficiency and demand.

Risk Analysis and Valuation

Considering that the company is unprofitable, the most suitable valuation metric is sales-related. With a P/S ratio of 7.1, which is below its historical median of 11.7, RBRK’s valuation position seems attractive.

In terms of EV/Sales, RBRK appears to be undervalued, reaching its low historical levels over the last year. Although RBRK has a D valuation grade on Seeking Alpha, we need to take into consideration that the comparison takes place with the information technology sector median, which includes many other companies not related to cybersecurity and data resilience, where RBRK operates.

The company’s main competitor is Commvault System (CVLT), which operates in the same business as RBRK: data resilience and recovery. For information and comparison purposes, add the main cybersecurity companies that may offer some features similar to Rubrik’s, such as agent security. These companies are PANW, CWRD, and the fast-growing SentinelOne (S).

As you can see, despite RBRK’s strong stock price decline, the company remains strongly overvalued compared to CVLT in terms of FWD PE. While, in terms of FWD revenue growth, RBRK is a expected to achieve the most significant growth, at 30.53% -almost double compared to CVLT’s 16.12%. Notably, RBRK is anticipated to have greater growth than traditional cybersecurity companies, as well as compared to S.

Risk factors are easy to identify. First, Rubrik is unprofitable and spends massive amounts on marketing expenses and SBC, increasing the risk of dilution and additional debt funding. Therefore, we are talking about a company with an operating model that is sensitive to macroeconomic fluctuations and competition.

Well established players, like PANW and CRWD, still focus on preventing and defending against cyberattacks, while RBRK targets data recovery; however, if any large player expands into this business line, it could put price pressure on Rubrik and effectively stress the company’s path to profitability.

Considering all the above, I believe Rubrik's risk-reward profile is not so attractive at current price levels. I would prefer to open a position after further price correction. Data recovery solutions have relatively limited room for expansion, while identity security seems to be the main future growth catalyst, and the RAC business is still at an early stage.

Given all the above, I rate the stock as HOLD.

data.title: Twin Disc's Pop Means It's Time For A Downgrade
data.description:
Whenever I rate a company a 'buy,' I am making the claim that the stock should outperform the market for the foreseeable future. When I am right, sometimes it's by a lot. And sometimes it's not. A good example of one company that has seen a bit of upside that has beat the market fairly comfortably, but that is now worthy of a downgrade is Twin Disc (TWIN). For those not familiar with the business, it focuses on producing and selling marine and heavy duty off-highway power transmission equipment. It does this through the manufacturing facilities that it has throughout the US, Belgium, Canada, Italy, the Netherlands, Switzerland, and Finland. Plus, the company has distribution locations across five other nations. This is pretty impressive for a firm with a market capitalization of just $222.5 million. And in recent years, we have seen an upward trajectory in the revenue of the company.

Since I called it a 'buy' back in October of last year, the stock has risen about 22%. That's not massive in and of itself. But considering that the S&P 500 is down 2.4% over that same window of time, I would take it. Management still seems optimistic about the future. And with the backlog on the rise, I don't see a reason not to be from an operational standpoint. Having said that, I do think that the easy money has been made and the current valuation of the company is more supportive of a 'hold' rating than it is a 'buy.' Because of that, I am downgrading the company accordingly.

Taking a fresh look at Twin Disc

Since my previous article about Twin Disc, investors have been given access to data covering two additional operating quarters. The most recent one is the second quarter of its 2026 fiscal year. According to management, revenue came in at $90.2 million. That's just a hair above the $89.9 million that the company reported a year earlier. According to management, the only reason for this rise in revenue was that the company had completed its purchase, in the third quarter of 2025, of Kobelt. This was a fairly small transaction, costing shareholders just $16.5 million. However, it boasts a global network of more than 120 distributors and systems integrators spread across 60 different nations. The goal here is to have this business offer up a complementary suite of motion control products for marine and industrial markets. This acquisition alone contributed $3.2 million in revenue on a year over year basis.

This was, unfortunately, largely offset by other factors. Management blamed tariffs and soft demand for oil and gas transmissions involving China for weakness in the commercial marine space. However, some of this weakness was offset by the company's Veth Propulsion systems, as well as by increased shipments of its Katsa Oy products that were acquired back in 2024. At its core, Veth Propulsion supplies main and auxiliary marine propulsion products to customers, with most of those sales coming from Europe, but a lot of growth in recent years is attributable to Asia and North America thanks to an emphasis on the luxury yachting market. During the quarter, Europe was also a source of strength for Veth, especially with its ELITE thruster products that have become rather popular amongst end users.

As revenue for the company improves, profitability has unfortunately been mixed. Net income actually skyrocketed from $0.9 million to $22.4 million. As great as this was to see, however, it is a bit of a mirage. The fact of the matter is that the company benefited from a $21.8 million income tax benefit for the quarter. Actual operating income declined from $2.8 million to $2.1 million. This came as marketing, engineering, and administrative costs, rose from 21.04% of sales to 22.90%. At first glance, that might not seem like all that significant of a change. But when applied to the revenue generated during the most recent quarter, it amounts to a decline in pre-tax earnings of about $1.7 million. The good news is that this weakness seems to have been temporary in nature. About $0.5 million stemmed from the firm's acquisition of Kobelt, while an equal amount came in the form of foreign currency fluctuations that negatively impacted financial results. Other profitability metrics dropped across the board. Operating cash flow plunged from $8.6 million to $4.5 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a much smaller decline, from $6.6 million to $6.4 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business contracted from $6.3 million to $4.7 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2026 compared to the same time in 2025. In this case, most of the metrics for the business moved higher. And according to management, that trend is likely to continue. Thanks to strong demand for its Veth Propulsion products, as well as a rise in global defense spending, the firm has seen its backlog expand. It went from $124 million in the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year to $175.3 million at the same time in 2026. Defense-related spending is particularly appealing, rising 18% sequentially for the quarter to now exceed $50 million. Management is also working hard on cost-cutting initiatives. A lot of this seems to be focused on countering tariffs. Examples would be optimizing its global footprint, including moving ARFF assembly to its Lufkin facility because that environment is more tariff advantaged. But now with the change in tariffs yet again because of the US Supreme Court, as well as new global tariffs, it will remain to be seen whether such a change is now set in stone. On top of this, the company is making other so-called operational enhancements, plus it is hiking prices on some of its items in order to boost profit margins.

Unfortunately, we don't have any guidance when it comes to the 2025 fiscal year as a whole. The only thing that we do know is that management said that there should be strong growth in the second half of this year, with the picture getting progressively better each quarter. Management has said in the past that their goal is to grow revenue from the $340.7 million it was back in 2025 to $500 million by the end of this decade. A lot of this will involve acquisition activities. But right now, we could only speculate on what that might look like. If the firm can achieve that revenue target, then margin expansion implies adjusted operating cash flow of about $33.5 million and EBITDA of $34.2 million. For context, last year, these metrics were $18.6 million and $19 million, respectively. And if we project out results based on what we have seen already for 2026, we would get readings of $23.9 million and $22.3 million, respectively.

If we use these estimates, as well as the results the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced at the moment. There are a couple of different ways to look at the picture. The first is through the lens of the firm in relation to other companies that have some similarities to it. In that chart, you can see how the company is valued in relation to these businesses. Only one of them ended up being cheaper on a price-to-operating cash flow basis. And when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, three of them were. I would argue that the more accurate way to value the business, however, would be to assume that it will continue to trade at the multiples it's currently trading for in 2030. The chart below shows what the total upside and the annualized upside would be in the event that management achieves these targets over this window of time. These numbers are lower than when I wrote about the company previously. And, quite frankly, they fall pretty much in line with what you would expect from the market more broadly. So I don't really see a reason that the business would outperform the market at this rate.

Taking a look at the big picture

Truth be told, reliable end market data for the company is a bit difficult to come by. Management really is not helpful in that regard. But we can piece together how things are going and what the opportunities and challenges for the company might be in the future. A lot of what the business does involves marine and propulsion systems for the commercial vessel market. The commercial vessel market was worth an estimated $96.72 billion last year. And by 2034, it should expand to about $172.94 billion. That's an annualized growth rate of 6.67%. It appears as though increased demand for oil, gas, and other energy products, will lead the way here. That rising demand necessitates an increase in construction in the market, and it also opens the door to the aftermarket sales that the company engages in.

Management mentioned in their latest earnings call that the defense industry is a growing opportunity for the firm. I already mentioned how, earlier in this article, the defense space grew 18% for the company in the most recent quarter, climbing to $50 million. In general, the defense sector does seem to be fertile ground for most businesses. According to one source, global top line defense spending is forecasted to rise to $2.6 trillion by the end of this year. And by the end of the decade, it should hit $2.9 trillion annually. The $2.6 trillion figure that has been forecasted represents an 8.1% increase over what was seen the year prior. And a large portion of that growth came specifically from the US military. Of course, this is not limited only to the marine propulsion space. It's defense in general. But as the saying goes, a rising tide lifts all boats.

Management is also optimistic, specifically when it comes to the hybrid and electrification market opportunities that are out there. When it comes to the marine space, the company has been making some investments in this respect. This includes marine control drives that allow vessels to achieve greater accuracy when it comes to positioning and slow-speed maneuverability; pleasure craft applications such as those involving its Hinckley SilentJet technology that helps to manage diesel and electric operation for the vessels in question; electric systems that reduce noise and provide more sustainable energy consumption than what traditional diesel engines offer; and more. But management is not focused on just the vessel market when it comes to the hybrid and electrification industry. On the industrial side of things, the business has offered up electronically managed and optimized clutch controls that enable electric fracking operations. Management believes that the marine, industrial, and off-highway markets will continue to see a transition toward these newer and greener technologies. And considering that there is the increased potential for between 5 and 10 times more content in this equipment than what we would see from more traditional equipment, there is certainly the opportunity for the company to capture some growth.

Having said that, there are certain challenges that investors should be aware of. First and foremost, we do seem to be heading toward an economic downturn. I have written about this in other articles like this one here. To the extent that a lot of what this prospect focuses on involves the defense sector, it will see some degree of stabilization, or even growth. It's also possible that it can continue to expand organically thanks to high quality innovations that are appealing to market participants. But at a minimum, certain economic data points are indicating that we are seeing some weakness. For just three of the last 40 months, for instance, manufacturing PMI data has indicated contraction. Real private fixed investment in the manufacturing space is actually down 14.8% for the final quarter of last year compared to the same time of the previous year. And in all likelihood, we should expect the picture to show further weakness. Another problem would be tariffs. Management recently made the case that tariffs elevated the cost of sales for the business by 3% in the final quarter of last year. But as I mentioned earlier in this article, management is already working to offset this through not only higher prices, but also certain footprint optimization activities as well.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that is currently available, it is my assertion that Twin Disc is still a solid company. But it's not one that I feel particularly bullish about anymore. The stock has appreciated while the market has dropped. Expectations for the future remain solid, but the gap that existed in terms of market expectations compared to what this business offered has been largely eaten up. Given this, I believe that downgrading it to a 'hold' is appropriate here.

data.title: Tap The Brakes And Buy SPLV
data.description:
Introduction

I am still looking for investments where I can, at once, fully participate in the equity markets and protect myself when the markets go haywire. I have been using models that incorporate 5-year, 7-year, and 19-year cycles. These models also include dampened curves to adjust to gradual increases or decreases in market trends over time. I wrote about my models last December. Based on these models, 2026 is the first year in a series of unfavorable investment periods. In both models, 2032 should mark the beginning of a favorable period. Additionally, I anticipate a volatile period in 2027 and 2028. It is time to adjust our investment strategies to assume these levels of risk.

Tapping The Brakes

One needs to be careful when making predictions about future market behavior. First, there is the issue of the margin of error, which ranges from about ±20% from one year to the next. Also, when I say we are in an unfavorable investment period, it does not mean there will be no positive gains. I am just saying that we are more likely to have a negative year. An unfavorable period means we will experience below-average returns over time.

Since we know we will see a volatile period, we need to find investments that allow us to remain in equities while also managing risk. With that in mind, I propose that investors consider buying Invesco’s S&P 500 Low Volatility ETF (SPLV).

SPLV Methodology

SPLV tracks the S&P 500 Low Volatility Index. We have data starting in 1990 that we can analyze. The basic methodology is to select the 100 S&P 500 securities with the lowest volatility (standard deviation). Here is the process:

Morningstar gives SPLV a 2-star (Neutral) rating. Lipper Leaders is a little more forgiving and gives the ETF a 3.4/5 rating (Quintile 1, 20th percentile). Zacks says SPLV is a hold. TipRanks gives it a middling 6/10 rating. Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha rates SPLV a sell (2.05/5).
