data.title: Clean Up Your Portfolio With Bath & Body Works
data.description:
I have been watching Bath & Body Works, Inc. (BBWI) for a good entry point for years. All the while, the stock valuation has continued to get cheaper and cheaper. Well, over the last month it appears the stock may be getting close to a bottom. In fact, the November spike low of $14.28 may provide support for price going forward. If this proves true, buying around the current quote of $18 and lower may be a smart play.

A month ago, management came out with lackluster guidance, but the stock quote may already be discounting the expected slowdown in calendar year 2026. The fact that new lows did not appear on weaker guidance could be telegraphing a turnaround is approaching.

Another plus for BBWI owners is the company reshored almost all production to Ohio from China in 2023. While ingredients and raw commodities for manufacturing its items are exposed to changing tariffs from President Trump, the company has become increasingly competitive on cost vs. other soap, lotion, and scent-focused producers.

Why is BBWI one of the least expensive retailers in America for an upfront valuation? It has to do with an excessive level of IOUs and repayment requirements, to the tune of $4 billion in financial debt and lease obligations, net of cash. The good news is less than $300 million in debt with an interest expense component is due during 2026-27. This compares to my estimate of repeatable free cash flow of around $600-700 annually as a run rate. Excluding close to $900 million in store leases into the future, below is the latest 10-K schedule of debt, with reasonable interest rates and maturities listed. During calendar year 2025, the company was able to both pay down debt and buy back a material number of shares.

As such, the company is not facing any immediate liquidity issues. Let's review the positive valuation backstop, and why now might be an opportune time to purchase a position.

Attractive Valuation

Seeking Alpha's Quant Valuation Grade is an overall "A" rating today. Why? On a variety of financial ratio comparisons to Bath & Body Works' 5-year trading history and relative numbers vs. current peer and competitor settings, BBWI represents one of the soundest values in retail.

Of particular note is the trailing EV to EBITDA ratio of 5.5x in April (boxed in gold above). When we include debt and equity totals (after backing out cash holdings) as full company value in a takeover, core cash EBITDA is being generated at a very high rate.

The full valuation on EBITDA is running near industry lows (retail and fragrance categories) presently. I have drawn this idea below, alongside a list of similar peer business models.

Enterprise valuation analysis also points to a buyout setup for new investors near the pandemic 2020 recession lows on EBITDA and sales. In essence, the stock may be discounting a mild recession already.

In terms of cash returns to shareholders on $18 for price, both the free cash flow yield of 22.3% (what's left for owners after investing in the business to keep it running) and the cash distribution yield of 4.3% are industry leading or nearly so. If debt doesn't become a major headache on slowing sales and/or a deep recession is avoided during 2026-27, Bath & Body Works looks quite interesting in my view.

Final Thoughts

Insider buying in shares really picked up at the end of 2025. Almost every director on the board purchased shares on the November implosion at prices around $15. Their confidence helped to pinpoint a price low, with a rebound taking place back to $24+ in February.

Sales and earnings are expected to dip lower this year, with analysts expecting a slight turnaround during 2028-29. In my mind, just a stabilization of financial results makes Bath & Body Works a sound purchase idea, because of high rates of cash return on investment for shareholders (FCF and dividend yields).

Investment Risks

I would recommend management focus on debt reduction with free cash flow coming in the door. The path to a dramatically stronger valuation is through less financial leverage. I suspect Wall Street would rerate share pricing higher to a valuation closer to peers if operating risks and margins are improved, a function of fewer IOUs.

Outside of the debt load, recession risk is the next worry for shareholders. A steep or long-lasting recession caused by a combination of negative trade/tariff effects on business/consumer confidence crossed with spiking oil/gas costs in March-April could push the economy into a contraction. With 95% of Bath & Body Works sales originating in the U.S. and Canada, a downturn in North America's economy could mean company revenue and income figures will fail to meet even meager expectations.

Excessive debt and lower sales are usually a toxic mismatch for any business. I suspect a share quote under $15 in 12-18 months would become reality given an oversized consumer pullback in overall spending patterns.

Upside Potential

The flip side is a bullish upturn in BBWI sales could lead to rather dramatic earnings gains and a stock quote well north of $30. If debt declines as a function of sales (hopefully compounding nominal debt reduction on the balance sheet), a higher valuation on rising results would really boost the share price.

For example, with a 10-year average P/E ratio of 10.5x, a premium ratio of, say, 12x EPS of $3.25 gets Bath & Body Works to $39 a share (the same price level as early 2025).

$39 a share in 12-18 months would be the equivalent of a total return (including dividends and share appreciation) of +120% in the not-too-distant future.

All things considered, including bearish and bullish outcomes, I rate Bath & Body Works a buy under $20. Assuming we avoid a catastrophic recession scenario, I peg Strong Buy territory around $15. At this stage, the lower the price goes, the more interesting the compounding potential becomes into 2027-28.

For sure, income and value investors should have BBWI on their research radar. It's a U.S.-based and focused company, with production and retail operations centered inside America, somewhat insulated from world events. A loyal customer core, friendly service inside each store location, and a decent value proposition on the goods available for purchase round out my upside arguments.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Devon-Coterra Merger: Good And Bad
data.description:
The merger between Devon Energy (DVN) and Coterra Energy (CTRA) will turn two mid-cap exploration and production (E&P) companies into a more diversified new large cap E&P company. The combined company is going to retain the Devon Energy name, and the ticker symbol will remain DVN.

From a proven reserves standpoint, the two companies are opposites. As of December 31, 2025, the two companies had similar proved reserves on a barrels of oil equivalent basis: DVN 2.4 billion, and CTRA 2.6 billion.

If we split this between liquids (oil and natural gas liquids) and natural gas, DVN's split was 69% liquids, and 31% natural gas. For CTRA the split was almost exactly opposite: 32% liquids and 68% natural gas.

The merged company will have pro forma combined proved reserves of 4.99 billion barrels of oil equivalent and a split of 49.96% liquids and 50.04% natural gas, a perfect balance if you have no preference between oil and liquids.

Since I have more of a preference for natural gas, I think this deal is good for Devon and bad for Coterra.

In 2025, DVN has production of 307 million barrels of oil equivalent, making its proved reserve to production ratio, or reserve life, 7.9 years. For CTRA, it had 286 million barrels of oil equivalent production in 2025, making its reserve life 9.0 years. The combined entity would have a reserve life of 8.4 years (593 mmBOE / 4,993 mmBOE). So this extends the life for DVN shareholders and shortens it for CTRA's shareholders. But the major factor is more the respective changes in the liquid and gas reserve splits.

From a production standpoint, at 2026 estimated levels, the new company would become the second largest E&P, after ConocoPhillips.

The bigger share of natural gas production in 2026 should protect Devon from expected declining oil prices this year. West Texas Intermediate futures markets are in quite heavy backwardation, and WTI is expected to close the year at around $75/bbl. Of course, if Middle East tensions flared up again, oil prices may spike again, but any narrative of $100/bbl plus oil prices being here to stay for 2026, I think, is unrealistic. The Economist wrote in a briefing following the recent ceasefire that "Mr Trump seems intent on ending the war, even though the Iranian regime remains in place and retains its ability to strike Israel with missiles and drones." With the midterm elections coming, the Trump administration is well aware of the impact of gasoline prices going into the midterm elections. The New York Times noted that Susie Wiles, the White House chief of staff, brought up the subject of gasoline prices, in one of the assessment meetings before starting the war in Iran.

Transaction Overview

The transaction is an all-stock deal, with an exchange ratio of 0.70 shares of Devon common stock for each share of Coterra common stock. At the time of writing, the market expects this transaction to close, as there is no merger arbitrage opportunity. With Devon trading at $47.4 per share and Coterra at $33.1 per share, the value of this transaction to Coterra shareholders is $47.4 x 0.7, or $33.2 per share, or a 0.2% premium, which is essentially a rounding error.

Efficient Operations

The merger creates a very efficient E&P company both in terms of drilling productivity and capital spent on a per-barrel of oil basis. Relative to the other top nine US-focused E&Ps, the combined DVN would place second or third in key operating metrics.

Shareholder Vote

Both boards have recommended the merger agreement, and the prospectus makes interesting reading on the background of the merger, which started in October 2025 with an unnamed publicly traded exploration and production company approaching Coterra to express interest in a combination.

Kimmeridge Energy Management, a private investment firm focused on the energy sector, published an open letter in early November pressuring Coterra's board to improve governance and strategic focus in November. The prospectus says Coterra officers held discussions with representatives of Kimmeridge. By the end of November, Coterra management had met with Devon, and negotiations began.

Fast forward to February 2026, and Kimmeridge wrote:

"As a significant shareholder in both companies, we are supportive of a combination that can unlock meaningful shareholder value. We continue to believe that will require portfolio rationalization and a renewed focus on the Delaware basin. Having formally submitted director nominees, we now eagerly await the disclosure of Coterra's slate, as well as the S-4 merger filing to better understand the competitive process its Board undertook to reach this outcome."

That Form S-4, as amended, was filed with the SEC on March 24, and the prospectus was filed on March 30.

Regulatory Clearance

According to the most recent 8-K filing as of this writing, "The closing of the merger is subject to, among other things, the satisfaction or waiver of certain conditions, including the expiration or termination of the applicable waiting period under the Hart-Scott-Rodino Antitrust Improvements Act of 1976." That was satisfied on April 1, moving the merger process forward, and now "the closing of the merger is expected to occur in the second quarter of 2026, subject to the satisfaction or waiver of other customary closing conditions specified in the Merger Agreement."

Risks

It is likely the deal will be consummated without much problem at the special meeting of stockholders on May 4th. I say likely because the merger arbitrage spread is effectively zero, indicating little risk the deal will collapse or that a new bidder will emerge.

The deal clears some of the issues we were worried about, at least the governance part, as the chair and CEO roles in the merged Devon will now be separated.

The deal also grows exposure to the Permian Basin's Delaware Basin, as Kimmeridge had been pushing for.

As always, there will be integration risk, as the merger transaction has "cherry picked" the best people to lead the executive leadership team, and of course, the makeup of the board of directors changes. On the merger conference call back in February, Devon's CEO Clay Gaspar said a couple of interesting things. First, on capital allocation, "I don't want to get too specific or ahead of my new pro forma board," and then on a question about acreage, he said:

We need to get to close. We need to consummate this deal. Then we need to really lay out what are those opportunities and capital reallocation and asset rationalization and then opportunities... I don't want to preempt which direction we're going to go. Obviously, we need to get together and really be able to talk about that and go deep on those opportunities.

So there is a risk that the board of directors, made up of six from Devon and five from Coterra, disagrees or takes time to decide on financial and operational strategy. The deal's $1 billion of stated synergies, which are above and beyond existing cost management programs at the respective companies, is always a risk, as it is not unusual for merger synergy projections to be off target.

Clearly, with a more balanced commodity mix, that reduces overall volatility of price realizations. However, the new Devon won't be attractive to investors who prefer oily pure plays or gassy pure plays.

My view is that oil prices will come down significantly but that natural gas prices will continue to rise, so if that's correct for Devon, this may net out, and then earnings growth becomes really reliant on achieving production volume growth.

Valuation

Devon trades on a P/E of about 11.5x, while Coterra trades at about 14.7x. I expect the merged entity to come out somewhere between 13x and 13.5x. This is a cheap valuation, given diversified E&P peers ConocoPhillips and EOG Resources trade at 19x and 15x, respectively.

Q1 Results

CTRA reports Q1 results on 30th April, and DVN reports on 5th May.

Conclusion

While this deal alleviates some of Kimmeridge's concerns around Coterra, from a longer-term perspective, as a natural gas bull, I'm sad to see gassy Coterra lose its independence. For Devon shareholders, this deal grows scale in the business, creates cost synergies, and in one fell swoop dilutes the oily exposure to become more balanced between liquids and gas. So it's a better deal for Devon shareholders. If Devon underperforms because of a falling oil price, or because the equity raise to fund this deal creates pressure on the stock price, and as some legacy Coterra shareholders sell their new Devon shares, on any Devon stock price underperformance, I would look to buy on weakness. Longer term, this transaction should be a good one for Devon shareholders.

data.title: Tyson Foods: Pivoting Beyond Cyclicality Into Structural Growth
data.description:
Introduction

Tyson Foods (TSN) went through a major investment cycle over the past years, aiming at becoming more than just a commodity company, which can prove to be a smart move, especially after we saw the recent volatility in poultry and beef.

TSN is a buy, as the company’s financials keep improving significantly while they stand to benefit from this internal move combined with a recovery in beef margins and the broader consumer/economic environment.

The Foundation Is Improving

TSN reported a solid quarter, navigating the ongoing macro pressure, including beef (which is expected to have a negative income in FY26) and the generally weak consumer environment.

Following a major post-pandemic investment cycle expanding their pivot towards higher value-added products like pre-cooked foods/snacks, Tyson Foods’ CAPEX has normalized, with the free cash flow reaching $1.18 billion in FY25, $1.46 billion in FY24, and -$187 million in FY23, expecting a strong FCF between $1.1 billion and $1.7 billion and $700 million to $1 billion in CAPEX in FY26, with 2% to 4% growth in sales, with a further drop in net interest expenses expected, to $370 million.

This comes as the company continues reducing their leverage, with a massive reduction to ~2.0x in Q1’FY26 compared to Q1’FY24, highlighting their disciplined approach in this environment while also returning solid amounts of money back to shareholders.

Financially, based on TSN’s latest report, we see a strong position overall, with the current assets covering their current liabilities well and a manageable amount of debt. Debt has been reduced YoY, recently pricing a new $500 million senior notes offering due in 2036 at a coupon of only 4.95%, which is not terrible in this environment, although this comes as a means to refinance part of their $800 million 4% notes due in March 2026.

Since the significant hit seen in 2023 due in part to the beef industry downturn and the massive margin compressions across their business, the company has managed to improve its balance sheet significantly despite the current pressure that’s still expected in this segment, with $1.4 billion of debt reductions over the past 12 months alone.

Currently, TSN pays a ~3.12% dividend yield, representing about $720 million per year (roughly 50% payout ratio based on the midpoint FCF guidance), while also repurchasing $47 million worth of stock during the quarter.

Iran Conflict Elevates Risk

Currently, despite the truce announced a couple of days ago, the Iran conflict remains the major near-term risk impacting the market, as we’re talking about something with implications that go far beyond the vital oil, LNG, or fertilizers.

This can further pressure the already weak consumer environment that’s been affected by the high-interest rates that naturally bring discretionary budgets down, with weak consumer sentiment, trade-downs, and high levels of stress reported not long ago, while even essentials like groceries saw softened spending intentions, according to a recent Deloitte report.

Although it may seem like a stretch now, we could even see the conflict’s effect on nitrogen-based fertilizers that’s pressuring the current planting season to affect future crop prices, which can in turn result in higher costs for farms and rising beef/chicken/pork prices as a result.

This is also mentioned by the deputy executive director of the World Food Program in a recent article, further highlighting how deep this conflict can hurt the roots of the global economy:

In the worst case, this means lower yields and crop failures next season. In the best case, higher input costs will be included in food prices next year.

This can further pressure the already weak US cattle herd, which is reportedly at a very concerning 74-year low following the significant droughts, so this is far from a great position.

As mentioned before, the potential inflation that can come out of this will likely play a role in the Fed’s next moves, with rate hikes being one of the central bank’s main tools in combating inflation. Another potential risk stemming from this comes from their $1.35 billion notes due in June 2027, which come at a coupon of only 3.55%. Despite only having $18 billion due in 2028, if we assume that they refinance this at a 5% rate, we're looking at a roughly $19.575 million increase in their interest, and it remains to be seen what they decide to do.

Meanwhile, a prolonged conflict can even spiral into a recession, and although the “we’ll still need to eat” argument is true, that doesn’t mean the company’s stock or its sales won’t be affected, especially when talking about their higher-margin or value-added segments.

Long-Term Potential Remains

Over the long-term, however, once the current macro pressure fades and the overall consumer sentiment improves, we could see the company’s investments towards the higher value-added products provide a significant boost, especially since they can benefit from ongoing macro trends in consumer behavior, which can be visible across all segments of the business.

In fact, this is among the companies that are best prepared for trends such as the protein “obsession,” GLP-1 adoption, or anti-ultra-processed policies, with their portfolio being superior to those of players that mostly sell frozen or canned food and snacks. This is also part of the reason why Mizuho initiated coverage on them recently with an Outperform, emphasizing their long-term potential to benefit from tailwinds:

We see Tyson well-placed to benefit from tailwinds (...) While investor skepticism persists about TSN’s ability to deliver consistent growth, and emerging geopolitical volatility presents short-term risks to consumer demand, we believe Prepared Foods is turning the corner on a structural basis

Although I noted the risk of private labels for many other food-related companies since these can allow retailers to capture the trade-downs seen as a result of the weak consumer environment (which can generate significant amounts of data that can be used to adapt to the evolving consumer demands and threaten legacy brands significantly), Tyson Foods is in a much better spot from this point of view.

In fact, thanks to their massive size, they’re considered a key supplier of Walmart’s (WMT) Great Value in-house brand, and Walmart is indeed TSN’s largest buyer (based on their 10-K report, they represented about 18.7% of their consolidated sales in FY25, which they say were included in “all the segments” of the company), although these are generally under strict NDAs, and you will rarely see a company brag about it. One of the few things that ties, or at least tied, them together for sure is an older USDA recall (from 2019) for Tyson Foods that actually listed some Great Value products as well. They also talked about this segment back during their Q4’FY25 Earnings Call:

Our retail branded products now reach nearly 72% of U.S. households, a rate that exceeds both private label and other branded competitors. Although private label sales are rising, their growth comes at the expense of other brands, not Tyson, as we continue to outpace the category in both volume and performance.

However, note that being a provider for private labels is not the most profitable business out there, and we saw bread producers talk about how low the margins can be there and look into cutting that segment of the business not long ago, including Grupo Bimbo’s (GRBMF) “strategic exits of some non-branded business.”

Risks Reiterated

Although the company has been investing significantly in their pivot towards higher-margin alternatives, the tough competitive environment and especially private labels can be a threat here as well. Sure, they can gain significant volumes by being a major supplier for these private labels, but the margin in that is not a business they’d want to chase, which is a growing risk, especially if the tough consumer environment continues.

Meanwhile, the Iran situation and its significant effects on the economy can be terrible for their higher-margin segments, while we have to remember that they’re also technically a commodity-adjacent company (despite their pivot into value-added products), which can lead to significant volatility that may be hard to pass to the final consumer at times such as these.

Valuation

For TSN’s DCF to Equity valuation, starting from the midpoint of their FCF guidance of $1.4 billion for FY26, I’ll assume no changes in net borrowings since their balance sheet seems to be at a very comfortable level after the major reductions seen already. I’ll assume a 7.5% CAGR over the first 5 years as a result of a potential recovery in their beef segment’s profitability (this alone can lead to much more than 7.5%), potential efficiencies and cost savings following their investments combined with improving consumer dynamics, followed by a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a 9.5% discount rate to take into account the macro- and commodity-related risks, as well as 354 million diluted shares outstanding as of their Q1'FY26 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $26.56 billion, for an intrinsic value of $75.02, which is still above the current $65.31 at the moment of writing this. Dividing the terminal value by the 10-year FCF gets us to a P/FCF of about 14.6, which seems very reasonable for this company, especially as their pivot advances.

Conclusions

TSN is rated a buy, backed by a strong and improving business that’s pivoting into more than just a commodity company, standing to benefit from a potential macro recovery as well.

This looks like a classic cyclical business, with financials rapidly improving following their investment cycle, ready to enjoy a potential turnaround once the consumer environment (and beef market) improves - which is not something they can control.
